questions.text: A patient has dysentery caused by a protist moving via pseudopodia. Which type of protist is this?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amoeboid protists like Entamoeba histolytica use pseudopodia for locomotion and phagocytosis.

questions.explanation: Amoeboid protists like Entamoeba histolytica use pseudopodia for locomotion and phagocytosis.

questions.text: A freshwater sample shows a unicellular protist with contractile vacuoles and chlorophyll a. Which feature best explains its survival in hypotonic water?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Contractile vacuoles expel excess water in hypotonic environments, preventing cell lysis.

questions.explanation: Contractile vacuoles expel excess water in hypotonic environments, preventing cell lysis.

questions.text: A researcher observes red and brown algae. Which pigment differentiates them?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Red algae contain phycoerythrin, and brown algae contain fucoxanthin, allowing absorption of different light wavelengths for photosynthesis.

questions.explanation: Red algae contain phycoerythrin, and brown algae contain fucoxanthin, allowing absorption of different light wavelengths for photosynthesis.

questions.text: A protist causes late blight in potatoes. Which group does it belong to?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phytophthora infestans, causing potato blight, is an oomycete, a fungus-like protist

questions.explanation: Phytophthora infestans, causing potato blight, is an oomycete, a fungus-like protist

questions.text: A biologist finds algae with red pigmentation thriving in deep water where blue light penetrates. Which pigment allows this?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phycoerythrin absorbs blue light, allowing red algae to perform photosynthesis at greater depths

questions.explanation: Phycoerythrin absorbs blue light, allowing red algae to perform photosynthesis at greater depths

questions.text: The shells of foraminiferans contain pores through which cytoplasmic projections are extended. These cytoplasmic projections help foraminiferans to
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Foraminiferans extend cytoplasmic projections (pseudopodia) through shell pores to capture and entangle prey for feeding. These projections also help in movement and anchorage.

questions.explanation: Foraminiferans extend cytoplasmic projections (pseudopodia) through shell pores to capture and entangle prey for feeding. These projections also help in movement and anchorage.

questions.text: Protists are considered as a paraphyletic group because they probably originated from
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Protists are considered paraphyletic because they likely evolved from more than one moneran (prokaryotic) ancestor, forming a group that does not include all descendants (like plants, animals, and fungi).

options.text: The heritable genetic changes in viruses
The organic substances of the environment

questions.explanation: Protists are considered paraphyletic because they likely evolved from more than one moneran (prokaryotic) ancestor, forming a group that does not include all descendants (like plants, animals, and fungi).

questions.text: Seaweeds is the common name used for
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Seaweeds commonly refer to brown algae, which are large multicellular marine algae like Laminaria and Sargassum. They contain the pigment fucoxanthin, giving them their characteristic brown color.

questions.explanation: Seaweeds commonly refer to brown algae, which are large multicellular marine algae like Laminaria and Sargassum. They contain the pigment fucoxanthin, giving them their characteristic brown color.

questions.text: Protists that form a monophyletic lineage with plants are
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ulva and Spirogyra are green algae that share a common ancestor with land plants, forming a monophyletic group. They have chlorophyll a & b and store starch, linking them closely to the plant lineage.

questions.explanation: Ulva and Spirogyra are green algae that share a common ancestor with land plants, forming a monophyletic group. They have chlorophyll a & b and store starch, linking them closely to the plant lineage.

questions.text: The disease that is caused by a microbe belonging to class apicomplexa is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Malaria is caused by Plasmodium, a protozoan belonging to class Apicomplexa, characterized by parasitic, spore-forming organisms. The other diseases are caused by bacteria or other protozoan groups, not Apicomplexa.

questions.explanation: Malaria is caused by Plasmodium, a protozoan belonging to class Apicomplexa, characterized by parasitic, spore-forming organisms. The other diseases are caused by bacteria or other protozoan groups, not Apicomplexa.

questions.text: The given illustrations show three types of protists. The illustrated protists exhibit diversity in terms of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amoeba (on the left) uses pseudopodia; Euglena uses flagella; and Paramecium uses cilia for locomotion, exhibiting diversity on the basis of locomotory structures.

questions.explanation: Amoeba (on the left) uses pseudopodia; Euglena uses flagella; and Paramecium uses cilia for locomotion, exhibiting diversity on the basis of locomotory structures.

questions.text: Given are some of the groups of protozoa I.Actinopods II.Apicomplexans III.Foraminiferans Pseudopodial mode of locomotion is found in group(s)
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option C is correct, as both actinopods and foraminifera use pseudopodia for locomotion

questions.explanation: Option C is correct, as both actinopods and foraminifera use pseudopodia for locomotion.

questions.text: Which protist genus is a classic example of mixotrophy, possessing both chloroplasts for photosynthesis and the ability to ingest organic material (heterotrophy) when light is unavailable?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Euglena is a classic mixotroph; it can perform photosynthesis using its chloroplasts but will switch to heterotrophy (absorbing organic nutrients or phagocytosis) in the dark.

questions.explanation: Euglena is a classic mixotroph; it can perform photosynthesis using its chloroplasts but will switch to heterotrophy (absorbing organic nutrients or phagocytosis) in the dark

questions.text: A massive 'red tide' (harmful algal bloom) is caused by an explosive increase in the population of which group of protists, and what is the primary structural characteristic used for their classification?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Diatoms: classified by their siliceous frustule (cell wall)
Foraminifera: classified by their perforated calcium carbonate shells
Dinoflagellates: classified by the presence of two flagella (one transverse, one longitudinal) and cellulose plates
options.explanation: Dinoflagellates are the main cause of toxic red tides and are uniquely characterized by their two flagella housed in grooves and cellulose plates.

options.text: Ciliates: classified by the presence of micronuclei and macronuclei

questions.explanation: Dinoflagellates are the main cause of toxic red tides and are uniquely characterized by their two flagella housed in grooves and cellulose plates.

questions.text: Which of the following characteristics best justifies the placement of oomycetes (water molds, e.g., Phytophthora) in Kingdom Protista rather than Kingdom Fungi, despite their filamentous hyphal growth and absorption of nutrients?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Their cell walls are made primarily of cellulose rather than chitin.
options.explanation: This is the primary biochemical distinction; Oomycete cell walls are cellulose-based (like plants), whereas true Fungi cell walls are chitin-based.

options.text: They exhibit a primarily diploid (2n) vegetative state.
They reproduce asexually by forming spores in sporangia.

questions.explanation: This is the primary biochemical distinction; Oomycete cell walls are cellulose-based (like plants), whereas true Fungi cell walls are chitin-based.
